data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Inflation Fell In March
data.description:
The good news is that consumer price inflation fell in March to a year-over-year rate of 2.4 percent.

This is down from a 2.8 percent, year-over-year, rate in February.

The drop and the size of the drop surprised almost everyone. Concerns were that inflation would rise in March.

Here is the recent history.

Yes, inflation seems to be coming down, but the inflation that has filled the 2020 calendar has been very excessive from the viewpoint of the Federal Reserve, which would like to see the overall inflation rate at something considerably less than current levels... something closer to 2.0 percent.

And the future seems to include a picture of even higher rates of inflation, given the movement in the world to a situation of much higher tariff rates.

An environment with higher rates of inflation is something that the Federal Reserve might have to deal with in the coming months.

Slowing economic growth may also be a component of the picture the Fed faces.

More and more analysts are painting a world of “stagflation” as what to expect in the near future... rising levels of inflation and slower economic growth.

How the Federal Reserve will react to this new picture is unknown at the present time.

For now, the Federal Reserve has just entered its third round of quantitative tightening, a regime that began with the “first round” getting started around the middle of March in 2022.

This third round of quantitative tightening began two weeks ago, and the Federal Reserve removed $18.4 billion from its portfolio in the banking week ending April 2, 2025, and actually allowed the securities portfolio to rise by $0.4 billion in the latest banking week, the banking week that ended on April 9, 2025.

In the second round of quantitative tightening, the Fed's securities portfolio declined by $257.0 billion. Also, in this second round, the Fed saw about $94.0 billion run off from its loan portfolio.

The Federal Reserve still would like to reduce its securities portfolio by about $2.0 trillion more, but it plans to do this further reduction at a slower pace than during the first and second rounds of the quantitative tightening.

But these plans may never see the "light of day" as the changing economy and the changing picture of government budget maneuvers may result in a future that no one can really see right now.

The quantitative tightening has been going on since the middle of March 2022.

Here is the picture of the reductions that have occurred in the Fed's securities portfolio during the past three years!

Notice that during this time, the securities portfolio has fallen from slightly more than $8.4 trillion to just about $6.4 trillion in the past banking week.

At the end of December 2019, the number of securities held outright by the Federal Reserve totalled just over $3.7 trillion.

So, one can make a strong argument, I believe that the Federal Reserve would still like to remove a substantial number of securities from its securities portfolio.

The amount the Fed would like to reduce the securities is unknown, but I feel that the number Fed officials would be shooting for is still relatively substantial.

Thus, quite a bit of work still remains to get the Fed's balance sheet back to where officials would like it to be.

In terms of the Fed's policy rate of interest, the Federal Funds rate, I believe that Chairman Jay Powell and the Federal Open Market Committee still believe that the time is not right for the Fed to reduce its policy rate of interest anymore in the very near future.

President Trump may not be very happy with this.

And as rising inflationary expectations work to push up interest rates, the Federal Reserve may be done...for the short run...with any near-term future moves in the policy rate.

Remember that the number one policy instrument in how the Fed conducts monetary policy these days is the expansion or reduction in the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright.

The Federal Reserve is on a “quantitative variable” approach to monetary policy.

Movements in the Fed's policy rate of interest follow.

The reluctance to move on adjusting the Fed's policy rate of interest is consistent with the current stance of making Federal Reserve policy.

Thus, I would be surprised if there is any move in the Federal Funds rate in the near future.

But...as mentioned in other parts of my writing...there is so much uncertainty floating around in the financial markets these days that investors must remain "loose and flexible" and keep their eyes open for what seems to be happening.

I believe that the Federal Reserve has done a very good job getting us to where the economy is these days.

My hope is that the Fed will have more time to continue its current policy of quantitative tightening.

I would like to see the Fed continue to reduce the size of its securities portfolio.

Personally, I would like to see the Fed reduce the size of the securities portfolio by another $1.5 to $2.0 trillion. I would also be very happy if the securities portfolio could be reduced by another $1.0 trillion.

I am not sure the Federal Reserve will have the chance to make these numbers. But they are my numbers and I will stand by them.

I believe that Chairman Powell is very intent on moving in this direction.

I believe that Chairman Powell is going to be pushed very hard away from this in the near future.

The history of this period will probably be a very, very interesting piece of reading.

Let's just hope that the outcome of the actions over the next several years does not end up with another major financial disturbance to explain.

data.title: Northland Power: Undervalued By The Market, Resilient To Tariffs And Positioned For Growth
data.description:
Northland power (TSX:NPI:CA) is a Canadian IPP focused on the development, construction, and operation of offshore wind farms. I believe that the recent downturn in the financial markets, triggered by the tariffs imposed by the Trump administration, may represent an excellent entry point for Northland. Indeed, the company has low exposure to the U.S. market, where it only owns a 220 MW onshore wind farm in New York State and has no projects under construction in the short to medium term. Moreover, the company is characterized by strong revenue stability, generating more than 90% of its revenue from contracted sources, a portion of which is indexed to inflation, making it resilient even in potentially adverse economic scenarios.

On the operational side, two huge offshore projects, Hai Long (1 GW) and Baltic Power (1.1 GW), will be completed between 2026 and 2027, which, with Oneida storage battery system, will boost revenue growth and operating income, adding, according to management estimates, about CAD 600 million in adjusted EBITDA and CAD 200 million in adjusted free cash flow. These developments would imply an FY 2027(e) adjusted EBITDA of CAD 1.8 billion, or about 6.5x in comparison to the company's EV.

Although Northland operates in a business characterized by high operational risks, I believe these are already priced in by the market and that the company has a potential upside of 50%, as evidenced by the DCF analysis. For these reasons, I assign Northland Power a "buy" rating.

Business Overview

Northland Power is an IPP with a net installed capacity of 2.84 GW as of December 2024, consisting of offshore and onshore wind farms, photovoltaic plants and gas production facilities. It generated 11 GWh of electricity in FY24, up 6.4% YoY (4% CAGR between FY19 and FY24). The company generates 60% of its revenue in Europe, 23% in Canada, 2% in the US and 15% in Colombia, where it operates through the regulated utility Empresa de Energía de Boyacá.

The company's operations are divided into 4 segments:

Offshore Segment

The Offshore segment had an installed capacity of 902 MW as of December 2024 and accounted for 50.2% of FY 2024 revenue, up 3.7% YoY due to increased electricity generation compared to FY 2023. Despite this, the business unit posted an EBIT margin of 46.6%, lower than the 47.43% recorded in FY 2023, as a result of higher maintenance costs attributable to the outage of an export cable at Gemini in June 2024, which led to a 16% increase in operating costs. The segment represents the company's main investment area and is currently engaged in the construction of two huge wind farms:

Hai Long, located offshore in Taiwan, will have an installed capacity of 1 GW. Completion is expected between FY 2026 and FY 2027, although it should start generating revenue as early as FY 2025. Northland holds a 30.6% ownership interest in the project but continues to take the lead role in the project's construction and operation. Hai Long 2A benefits from a 20-year PPA with Taipower, while Hai Long 2B and 3 have secured a 30-year PPA with an investment grade counterparty (S&P: AA-). The total cost of the project is around CAD 9 billion, of which CAD 5 billion is financed by non-recourse debt, CAD 1 billion from pre-completion revenue, and the remainder through equity investments by the project partners (Northland, Gentari, and Mitsui & Co). Northland's share in the project is expected to generate average adjusted EBITDA between CAD 230 and 250 million.

Baltic Power, located offshore in Poland, has an installed capacity of 1.1 GW and is expected to be completed in FY26. Northland holds a 49% ownership in the project, through a JV with Orlen (51%). The cost of the project is around CAD 6.5 billion and will be remunerated through a 25-year euro-pegged and inflation-indexed CfD with the Government of Poland. Northland's share in the project is forecast to generate an average adjusted EBITDA of between CAD 300 and CAD 320 million.

Onshore Segment

The Onshore business had installed capacity of 968 MW of onshore wind and 247 MW of solar farms as at December 2024. The business unit accounted for 20.3% of Northland's FY 2024 revenue, up 9.9% YoY as a result of the full-year contribution from New York wind projects and higher wind resources, partially offset by lower solar resources in Spain. The segment recorded an EBIT margin of 38.78% in FY 2024, compared to 39.38% in FY23.

In March 2024, the company completed the sale of the 130 MW La Lucha solar facility, for consideration of CAD 205 million. The sale is in line with the strategy of developing onshore assets with the aim of selling them, raising capital, or use the cash generated to finance the development of offshore assets, which require a significant amount of capital but are characterized by higher expected returns.

Northland is completing the construction of the 250 MW Oneida storage system in Ontario, with the facility due for completion in FY25. The project, roughly 70% controlled by the company, generates 60% of its revenue from a 20-year contract with Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator; the remaining part of the revenue will come from electricity selling at market price. The estimated cost is CAD 800 million and is expected to generate around CAD 40 million of average adjusted EBITDA.

Efficient Natural Gas and Utility Segments

The Efficient Natural Gas segment has an installed capacity of 723 MW and generated 13.5% of the company's revenue in FY24, down 6.4% YoY due to lower natural gas prices and reduced operating availability as a result of plant outages. This segment is the only one with a steady decrease in installed capacity and, hence, in revenue. Despite this, the growing demand for electricity has prompted Northland's management to change its strategy, expecting new strategic investments in the segment. As early as November 2024, it completed a 23 MW capacity expansion at the Thorold gas plant in Ontario, with a CAD 40 million investment.

The decision to pursue new investments in gas facilities will enable the company to leverage its experienced team of gas professionals, using short-term capital to generate cash flows that will fund the development of long-term offshore renewable projects (e.g. ScotWind). The recent appointment of Christine Healy as CEO has given new impetus to this approach. Indeed, in the Q4 conference call, the new CEO highlighted the relevance of gas to meeting the rising demand for base load capacity caused by drivers including data centers, reshoring, and electrification.

Northland acquired Colombian regulated utility EBSA in 2020 with approximately 480,000 customers and supplying 1.8 TWh of electricity annually. The segment accounted for 15.1% of Northland revenue in FY 2024, up 18% YoY. The business unit is the fastest-growing segment in which the company operates, with enhanced performance being primarily driven by growing regulated asset base and rate escalations. These factors led to a steady improvement in operating margins, with the EBIT margin at 32.19% in FY 2024, from 29.12% in FY 2023 and significantly higher than the 24% in FY 2020, the year of acquisition.

What Makes Northland a Compelling Bet Amid Current Economic Conditions

As of April 2, 2025, Northland's market cap declined by approximately 10%. In spite of that, the company's business model is resistant to the impact of tariffs. That is because Northland generates only 2% of its revenue in the US, through an onshore wind farm that is already operating in New York state and owns no projects under construction in the short to medium term, thus requiring no imports into the US. Hai Long and Baltic Power will not require US components as most wind blades, towers, and turbines needed to come from Asia and Denmark, respectively. Even for Alberta's Jurassic solar project, which is in advanced stages of development, components will be imported from Asia.

In the event tariffs lead to a worldwide economic slowdown or recession, Northland would experience little impact on revenue, margins, and cash flows, as over 90% of its revenue is contracted. Additionally, any decline in interest rates due to bad economic conditions could even turn out to be an opportunity as the company would be able to refinance loans with higher interest rates.

Furthermore, the completion of Hai Long, Baltic Power, and Oneida will drive new momentum in revenue and margin growth. Management estimates a cumulative adjusted EBITDA of CAD 600 million from the three projects, forecasting adjusted EBITDA between CAD 1.6 and CAD 1.8 billion in FY27, about 30% higher than in FY24. The two offshore projects will also positively impact margins and profitability, as they feature higher, expected, returns than the onshore ones. This is reflected in the offshore segment’s EBIT margin of 46.6% in FY24, significantly above the 38.78% reported by the onshore segment.

Northland, contrary to some IPPs, is already generating positive free cash flow despite significant investment activity. In FY24, it generated FCF of CAD 651 million (including the sale of La Lucha). The completion of the new projects will contribute an additional CAD 200 million in adjusted FCF, allowing the company to keep debt under control (already stable over the FY20–FY24 period) and to maintain a high level of shareholder remuneration, with a dividend yield of 6.56%. Management also announced that starting with the April 2025 dividend, the dividend reinvestment plan will be executed through open market purchases, minimizing shareholder dilution.

In my opinion, a final factor that makes Northland an attractive investment is its low valuation, in fundamental terms, compared to some of the major peers. The comparison provided by Seeking Alpha with Ormat Technologies (ORA), ReNew Energy Global (RNW), Enlight Renewable Energy (ENLT), Clearway Energy (CWEN) and NextEra Energy (NEE) highlights Northland's relative undervaluation compared to its competitors. Indeed, the Canadian company shows a lower P/E, EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA than all the other companies under review.

Main Risks

Northland operates in a highly regulated and subsidized industry. Regulatory changes in its main countries of operation (Germany, Netherlands, Canada and Colombia) may negatively impact its bottom line.

Northland generated 15.1% of its revenue and 11.9% of its adjusted EBITDA in Colombia in FY24, a country characterized by high political risk. In addition, operations in the country also entail a risk of exposure to currency volatility relative to the Canadian dollar.

Dealing with the construction of large projects, the biggest operational risk for the company is poor planning, which can lead to delays in project completion, cost overruns, and unexpected technical challenges.

The war in Ukraine has simultaneously highlighted the centrality and fragility of energy infrastructure. Though the risk is low for the moment, an eventual war between China and Taiwan could pose a very high risk to the Hai Long offshore wind farm, with a significant impact on Northland's future economic performance.

Northland, which deals with large offshore wind farms, requires substantial amounts of capital. An inability to raise it or high interest rates could negatively impact its business model.

Discounted Cash Flow

To attribute a valuation to Northland Power, I performed a three-stage DCF. I made an estimate of the expected values of revenue, EBIT margin, CAPEX and depreciation for FY 2025, FY 2026, FY 2027. I made a projection of these values for the period between FY 2028 and FY 2034, finally calculating the terminal value. This analysis returned a target price of CAD 26.66 per share, which is about 50% higher than the current market price. To determine this value, I made the following assumptions:

Beta was obtained using the unlevered beta of "Green & Renewable Energy" industry reported by Professor Aswath Damodaran, subsequently calculating levered beta based on NPI's D/E ratio. A value of 0.96 was derived.

Cost of debt of 3.5%.

Risk-free rate of 4.92%, based on the average of the 10-year government bonds of the countries where NPI operates.

Equity risk premium of 4.29% was obtained from the weighted average of the ERPs of the countries where NPI operates. Countries' ERPs are provided by Aswath Damodaran. Cost of equity of 9.02% derived from CAPM model.

WACC of 6.3%.

The growth rate (g) of 2%.

Takeaway

I believe that Northland Power's stock price has been excessively penalized by investors, as the company has minimal exposure to tariffs and is covered by more than 90% contracted revenue in the event of an economic slowdown or recession. The company continues to invest significant amounts of capital in offshore projects, diversifying its renewable assets and reducing their concentration. The completion of Hai Long and Baltic Power will lead to a considerable increase in operating margins and cash flows, enabling the company to keep debt under control, gradually increase its dividend, and begin the development and construction of new offshore projects, a sector in which it is among the top players worldwide. Considering the results of the DCF analysis, which indicate a potential upside of 50%, I assign a "buy" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UTG: Big Steady Income, Healthy AI Price Pullback
data.description:
The Reaves Utility Income Trust (NYSE:UTG) is a special utility sector-focused closed-end fund offering big, tax-advantaged, monthly income (7.6% yield) and unique exposure to the AI datacenter megatrend, which has just pulled back and thereby offers some compelling price appreciation potential (not to mention it also trades at a small but attractive discount to net asset value (NAV). This report reviews the fund’s strategy, holdings, distribution safety, valuation (and megatrend exposure) and risks, and then concludes with our strong opinion on investing.

Overview

With an inception date in 2004, and according to the fund’s website, the objective of UTG is (emphasis ours):

“To provide a high level of after-tax income and total return, consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation. The Fund pursues this objective by investing at least 80% of its total assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry. Up to 20% of the Fund may be invested in the securities of companies in other industries.”

And as you can see in the chart above, UTG has accomplished this objective by delivering steady growing monthly income distributions (dividends) to investors through a combination of long-term capital gains (taxed at the lower long-term capital gains tax rate, depending on your personal tax situation) and qualified dividends from its recently 59 underlying holdings (qualified dividends are also eligible for the lower qualified dividend tax rate, unlike some other CEF distributions, and depending on your personal tax situation—such as what is your income tax rate, and if you hold the shares in a taxable account or in a tax-advantaged individual retirement account).

As mentioned above, at least 80% of UTG’s total assets must be invested in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry. Here's a look at the current holdings breakdown (as per UTG’s most recent quarterly fact sheet).

Specifically, the fund had 60.6% of its holdings in the Utilities “industry.” This may seem to violate the 80% utility industry rule mentioned above, but per a previous voice message we received from the company:

"Hey,... calling from Reaves Asset Management, we're the advisor of the Reaves Utility Income Fund. Appreciated your question on utility allocations... We use a utility definition that is a little looser for the bulk of the assets as described in the prospectus."

For more color, you can see the fund’s recent top 10 holdings below (many of which have attractive exposure to the AI/datacenter megatrend—more on this later).

(TLN) (VST) (CEG) (NI) (EPD) (PPL) (ETR) (PNW) (TXNM)

Also, for more perspective, here's a look at the fund’s recent holdings breakdown across stocks, bonds and cash.

You’ll note in the graphic above, cash is negative, indicating leverage (or borrowed money), a topic we will cover more later in this report.

7 Big-Yield CEF Questions to Always Ask

Before investing in any big-yield CEF, here's a list of seven specific questions you may always want to ask yourself.

Does UTG Match Your Investment Goals:

Every investor is unique with their own individual goals and situation. UTG can help a lot of investors achieve their goals if the fund’s objectives (i.e., to provide a high level of after-tax income and total return consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation) match their personal situation.

For example, if you like big, steady monthly income payments, you might consider owning some UTG.

However, it’s important to consider the fund’s specific holdings and megatrend exposure. For instance, in UTG’s case:

Utility Sector: Traditionally, the utilities sector is known for relatively low-price volatility and relatively high dividend income. This is because utility companies (such as the electric company that allows you to keep the lights on) are a steady business (even when the economy gets bad—people still need electricity to keep the lights on, charge their devices, etc.). So, in this sense, UTG is a steady business, and that's further supported by the fund’s relatively low three-year beta of 0.59 (beta is a measure of market risk exposure, whereby the S&P 500, for example, is the baseline with a beta of 1.0, and anything below 1.0 is considered less volatile as compared to the rest of the market, per se).

AI/Datacenter Megatrend: Investors should also be aware of the fund’s exposure to the AI megatrend through datacenter electricity demand. Specifically, the utilities sector is expected to grow significantly over the next decade (a good thing) because of the increasing demand for electricity needed to power AI, which is run through offsite cloud-based data centers. To give you an idea of just how large this growing demand is, the following chart suggests datacenter electricity demand (as a percent of total US electricity demand) will grow from around 5% to around 12% over the next decade. This is huge and good for UTG considering it owns names like Talen, Vistra and NiSource (see our earlier top 10 holdings chart).

Distribution Safety:

Investors can get a feel for the safety of UTG’s distribution first by recognizing its consistency (and growth) since the fund’s 2004 inception (see our earlier distribution history chart).

Second, the table below shows the majority of the distribution is from income (received from the funds underlying holdings) and capital gains (again on the fund’s underlying holdings) and includes only a small amount of ROC (return of capital) recently, which is basically when the fund returns some of an investor’s investment dollar just to maintain the distribution (which is totally fine, from time to time, as long as it is not excessive—which it has not been).

Further still, you can see the ongoing dividend growth of the fund’s underlying holdings, using this link, which shows, on average, the underlying holdings increased their dividend payouts by over 10% last year—a good thing!).

Price Discount to NAV

Unlike traditional mutual funds and exchange trade funds, CEFs can trade at large premiums and/or discounts to the aggregate value of their underlying holdings (because there is no immediate mechanism in place to prevent this), and as such, this can create significant risks and opportunities.

All else equal, we greatly prefer to purchase attractive CEFs when they are trading at a discount to NAV, which UTG currently is (a small one of ~1.1%).

And for a little more perspective, here's a look at UTG’s share price and dividend paid over time.

As you can see above, the dividend has grown steadily over time (it now sits at $0.19 per share each month) and the price has also grown (although recent price volatility has created a more attractive buying opportunity, especially considering the ongoing utility sector megatrend related to datacenter/AI and considering the underlying holdings have continued to increase their dividends paid over time, especially over the last year, as mentioned earlier).

Risks:

Before investing in UTG, investors should also consider the risks.

Leverage: Closed-end funds frequently use a little leverage (or borrowed money) to increase the distributions and total returns over time. This can be attractive in the good times, painful in the bad times, but potentially very good over the long term.

UTG recently had around 19.6% leverage, which is very reasonable considering the low volatility and high dividend payout of the fund (remember, utility sector stocks tend to be less volatile, and UTG’s beta is still well below 1.0, even including the leverage).

Further still, the shares have sold off recently (perhaps a bit more than they would have if there was no leverage). And while this creates some pain for existing investors, it also creates a more attractive entry point for new investors, in our opinion.

Expenses: CEF expenses are a common complaint of CEF investors. For example, some investors are turned off by UTG’s recent expense ratio of ~2.43%.

Important to note, this expense ratio includes the cost of leverage and other operating expenses, plus the investment advisor’s fee. In our view, the total expense ratio of this fund is meaningful but reasonable and acceptable considering the strategy.

The management team is another factor (risk) for investors to consider. However, in the case of UTG, we view the management team as reasonably good stewards of capital. For example, they have a well-defined investment philosophy and disciplined investment process. And the management team has an average of 20 years of experience.

The Bottom Line

We consider UTG attractive enough to rank it in our new report: Top 7 Big Yields (CEF and BDC Edition). In particular, the current market selloff has pulled down the price, yet its distribution and holdings remain steady and healthy.

Additionally, UTG trades at a small discount to NAV (attractive), it has exposure to the AI/datacenter megatrend (electricity demand is growing) and it's significantly less exposed to market volatility (it has a low 0.59 beta) as compared to other opportunities (potentially a very good thing in the current market environment, and if you're simply at a point in life where you prefer to focus on steady income above long-term volatile price appreciation).

UTG is currently a significant holding in our 23-position High Income Now Portfolio at our Investing Group service, Big Dividends PLUS.

At the end of the day, you need to do what is right for you. And if you're a disciplined, long-term, income-focused investor, UTG is absolutely worth considering.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Costco Amidst Tariff Risks: To Have Or To Hold?
data.description:
Thesis

For the membership-based warehouse supermarket leader Costco Wholesale Corporation (NASDAQ:COST), the potential impacts of President Trump's tariffs leave more questions than answers, and investors should carefully navigate the period ahead as recession risks remain, and retaliatory tariffs threaten to impact supply chains, especially from China, and product inflation. Costco is considered a defensive stock, as most of its revenue is derived from less discretionary purchases, but it will not be immune from tariffs entirely.

With the company priced highly – in relation to its peers and its historical averages – and with growth in revenue expected to stagnate somewhat, I view COST shares as presently overvalued. Costco is a hugely successful business with a loyal customer base, and investors with a long-term horizon will continue to enjoy decent returns, but I expect to see a more favorable entry point emerge soon. I rate shares a hold.

Company Background and Performance

Costco has been a bellwether for American economic growth for decades. It has a long track record of cash generation as a business and enjoys many competitive advantages: a profitable business model based on both product sales and membership fees; massive economies of scale; and a strong brand and loyal customer base. While it does not enjoy a huge amount of pricing power in relation to its consumer goods – given that big box retail stores and supermarkets operate in a very competitive marketplace where customers look for value and closely track prices – it does enjoy enormous pricing power for its membership fees, from where it derives much of its profit. This positions it in a different space than regular retail stores and supermarket chains, where margins are thin. It has dominant power in this specific sub-section of consumer spending.

Costco's investors with a long-term focus have been richly rewarded, and I expect they will continue to be over coming years and decades. Investors have demonstrated trust in the management and shown they are willing to pay a premium for a company that takes the stress away from investing, and a look at its stock chart over an extended period shows this clearly.

The stability of their business and reassurances of consistent profitability – even if a low to mid-teens gross margin – comes at a cost, however. Costco is richly valued, with a P/E ratio of 53, a forward PE of 47.9 and EPS growth rates in the very low double digits, it has a current PEG ratio of 5.4. This positions it above its peers – Walmart (WMT), Kroger (KR) and Target (TGT) – but each of them has higher gross profit margins; Costco's is at 12.7% compared to the mid/high-20s for the others. Moreover, both Kroger and Walmart have superior EPS growth prospects according to analysts.

The impact of Trump's sweeping tariffs has added further factors for investors to consider here. With so many "moving parts," investors are wise to assess possible outcomes before jumping head-first into owning equity in any business right now, even a consistently profitable defensive stock like Costco.

Potential Tariff Impacts

I see two potential outcomes from Trump's tariffs. Firstly, Costco will be forced to raise its prices but, theoretically, not across the board and instead focused only on certain items. In fact, Reader's Digest recently reported several consumables subject to higher prices.

According to CNN, imported food items represent approximately 17% of American consumption, but in certain areas, significantly more: 80% of seafood; 80% of coffee; 59% of fresh fruit; and 35% of vegetables. The U.S., due to crop and climate issues, cannot hope to produce every food item that American consumers want to eat, so to think that American farmers can produce mangoes or catch shrimp at the high volumes necessary to meet American consumption needs is simply unreasonable. If Americans want to continue having their rich variety of food options, international trade is necessary. Tariffs will hurt in this regard, and force Costco and its peers to either raise prices, leading to customer churn or spending reductions, or accept lower profit margins.

Impacts would be spread across both its (non-discretionary) grocery and (discretionary) houseware businesses. Regarding the former, Costco is in a very favorable position compared to smaller, less agile peers. It can potentially absorb wholesale price increases with its strong product mix. Costco could theoretically take a loss on certain items, knowing that it can make this up somewhere else. The CEO Ron Vachris stated in the latest earnings call:

...our people are very well equipped to lower prices and defer any cost increase that come our way.

He also reiterated their strong relations with suppliers, which they can leverage better than their smaller competitors. This means that they could continue to attract customers and steal market share from local grocers, who have less flexibility in their pricing models. Over the long term, it is possible that tariffs improve their market share by crippling their smaller competitors.

Costco's exposure to Chinese imports, especially in the household items, means they are not entirely protected, however. Recently, Costco asked its suppliers in China to cut prices, which represents another lever they can pull. According to Vachris, less than half of the 1/3rd of company sales that come from imported products – so, less than 1/6th of product revenue – derives from China, Mexico and Canada.

While products from China are currently subject to 145% import tariffs (as of April 11), many products from Mexico and Canada are exempt from the 25% tariffs on imports from these two countries. This includes food and agricultural products, as they are covered by the existing USMCA free-trade agreement. So, when you look closely, the proportion of products likely impacted by tariffs from China is probably less than 10%, though the proportion from other countries – and subject to the sweeping 10% tariffs that exist right now – is likely higher.

In the latest earnings call, Vachris stated in his prepared remarks:

It is difficult to predict the impact of tariffs, but our team remains agile and our goal will be to minimize the impact of related cost increases to our members.

They have a number of options at their disposal to do so, but some degree of price inflation – which may hurt consumer spending – and falling margins is to be expected. While Costco is positioned strongly in this challenging macroeconomic environment, they are certainly not immune to tariffs.

A second possible outcome is that the tariffs lead to a recession, which, when mixed with inflation – and, therefore, an inability for the Fed to lower interest rates – could also lead to rising unemployment and an economy characterized by stagflation. This would be a terrible outcome, and the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930 – signed by President Hoover, amidst bipartisan opposition, to protect American jobs in manufacturing and farming – serves as a cautionary tale for what could happen. Reciprocal tariffs led to a 70% global slowdown of trade; businesses were crushed, families were cast into poverty, markets tumbled. Such a result in 2025/26 is not beyond the realms of possibility, though the President does have the option of reversing course, albeit with some sustained erosion of general "trust" in the U.S. as a global trading partner.

Speaking of reciprocal tariffs, the Chinese recently responded with 125% tariffs on U.S. goods imported to China. Aside from the potential global slow-down of trade that this tit-for-tat competition between the U.S. and China may accelerate, there is also the question of how these reciprocal tariffs will impact American businesses that sell to the Chinese. Costco has seven stores in China – representing less than 1% of its 903 in total – so any material reduction in revenue would be minimal. Without knowing exactly what proportion of goods sold in these stores is based in the U.S. – though I suspect somewhere in the region of 20-40% – this will almost certainly hurt the company's same-store sales.

Also, investors should anticipate growing anti-U.S. sentiment among consumers in China and elsewhere. Costco has 280 stores outside of the U.S. (incl. 109 in Canada and 41 in Mexico), which constitute 31% of revenues. They will not be immune to the shifting winds of socio-political impacts and consumer spending if a revolt/boycott of sorts materializes.

Conclusion

Costco stock has traded better than the S&P 500 (SP500) of late, driven by the "flight to safety" that often precedes a likely recession or follows a growing collective fear of one. This may provide some stability to the share price going forward, but I would expect it to remain under pressure given its historically high valuations, seen in its five-year P/E-ratio chart below.

Any sign that Costco is facing pressure on its profit margins would likely lead to another drawdown. While its solid balance sheet and wide moat will protect the company over the long term, I do not view shares as good value right now. I rate Costco a hold.

data.title: BlackRock Q1: Achieved Strong Results In A Tough Environment
data.description:
I rate BlackRock, Inc. (NYSE:BLK) as a Buy, for capital appreciation focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. Although the start of the year hasn't been ideal for an asset manager, the long-term prospects for BLK are very positive. Therefore, the drop in stock price of 15% year to date presents an opportunity to invest at a better value. The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Strong Q1 results, showing revenues up +12% and earnings up +15%
  • Current dividend yield of 2.43%, with a history of increasing the dividend over time.
  • Strategic growth generated from private markets business, creating more profitable revenue.

Full disclosure, I own this stock at $990.70 cost basis, so my position is down 12.6%. The reason I'm holding the stock, in fact, buying more, is based on its future as a financial services leader. BLK is very good at what they do, in my opinion, are finding new streams of revenue, and are delivering on key metrics such as revenue growth and earnings.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold 3.13 (as of 4/11/2025) due to the declining stock price year to date.

Leading Global Investment Manager

BlackRock's stated purpose is:

to help more and more people experience financial well-being. As a fiduciary to investors and a leading provider of financial technology, our clients turn to us for the solutions they need when planning for their most important goals.

The company is best known for its ETFs under the iShares name, which are easily accessible to the average investor. They recently bought into alternatives such as infrastructure and private credit, which is fueling growth.

BLK recently bought Prequin, a leading provider of information on the private markets, including such investment products as private equity and private credit. Larry Fink, the CEO, has stated his ambition is to bring private markets to the average retail investor, perhaps allowing them to invest in their retirement accounts. Usually, private market investments are only open to accredited investors, requiring a net worth of $1M excluding a primary residence, and income of $200K. Last year, BLK bought Global Infrastructure Partners and is currently closing on the acquisition of Global Infrastructure Partners.

Q1 2025 Earnings Achieved Results

On April 11, the company declared Q1 earnings, which reinforced the reasons to own the stock. Overall takeaway is the BLK is handling the volatility of the markets well, growing with key strategic businesses such as the actively managed funds and private markets business which will outperform over time. Financial highlights for the quarter include:

  • Revenue of $5.28 billion, up 11.6%
  • Adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $11.30 up 15%
  • Assets under management (AUM) grew to $11.58 trillion, up 11%.
  • $84 billion of quarterly net inflows
  • 16% growth in technology services and subscription revenue, leveraging the Aladdin platform and Prequin data.
  • 6% organic fee growth, achieving goal of 5%.
  • Adjusted operating margin of 43.2%.
  • $7 billion in inflows driven by private market investments.

BLK Outperforms the S&P 500 Index Over Time

A $10,000 investment in BLK made 10 years ago would be worth $29,940 today, exceeding the return of the S&P 500 (SP500) as shown below by iShares S&P 500 Index Fund (BSPPX).

Valuation

From 2003 to present, the dividend history shows a consistent growth of distributions, which is very attractive to long-term buy and hold investors. Simply stated, if an investor holds the stock, the dividend payout will rise over time, and so will the stock price.

Breaking down the dividend further, in the chart below, the trends over time look positive. First, the dividend payout amount is rising over the past 10 years. The dividend growth is consistent over time. Second, the dividend yield is averaging just over 2%, which means the stock price is going up along with the rising dividend.

The strength of the dividend is especially important in volatile times because it serves as a base for the stock price. If the stock goes down, the dividend becomes more appealing. Therefore, the price will go back up as investors see a good value. Further, if the stock is down, current stockholders are rewarded for waiting with dividend income, creating an incentive not to sell.

In comparing BLK vs. peers, we can see the entire sector is performing. Although the comparison is not exactly apples to apples, all companies are managing assets. Blackstone, Apollo, and Ares are heavily involved in alternative assets or private markets, which in recent years have been a more profitable business than public equities. BLK is smart, in my view, for going after a slice of the alternative business.

Blackstone Inc. (BX) offers the best dividend yield of the group at 4.52%, along with strong dividend growth and 5-year total return metrics.

Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO) has the least attractive dividend payout but a strong 5-year total return.

Ares Management Corporation (ARES) offers a 3.3% dividend yield with the best 5-year dividend growth CAGR, and the best 5-year total return of 351%.

State Street Corporation (STT) is the least appealing of the group, with the lowest 5-year total return.

Outlook

Short term, volatility in the markets is unfavorable for BLK, as they make money from fees as a percentage of AUM. If the market goes down, they make less money in fees, more clients will sell positions, keeping cash on the sidelines. If the tariff war was negotiated and ended up favorably, the market might spring back up, creating higher fees on AUM.

Longer term, BLK has core competencies that will serve to drive value. The scale of their ETF business in iShares combined with the growth potential in their alternatives business will continue to create growth upon growth. In my opinion, the management team is very strong under CEO Larry Fink, with a proven history of achieving extraordinary results. This is likely to continue.

Risks To Rating

The greatest risk to BLK is a declining market caused by the tariff war or overall recession. As events cause investors to sell and move to cash, the lower market values create lower fees on AUM. Tariffs also have the added negative effect of lowering company earnings as they face higher costs of production. For example, as the Apple Inc. (AAPL) iPhone is currently being made in China, which puts stress on the supply chain, not to mention potentially doubling the costs of shipping it back to the U.S.

Bottom Line

I rate BLK as a "Buy" for capital appreciation focused investors looking for long-term buy and hold investments. BLK just declared Q1 earnings, posting strong results in revenues and earnings. While the short term could experience volatility, longer term I am certain BLK will emerge a winner in markets given the strength of their business model, strong management team, and strategic acquisitions in the alternative investment space.

The dividend currently pays a 2.43% yield on a quarterly basis. While on the surface this seems low, the company has a long history of consistently raising the dividend, which has the added effect of raising the stock price.

The main risks to the company are the current tariff war which puts pressure on markets, lowering fee revenue coming from AUM. On the other hand, the growth potential of alternative markets has the potential of reducing volatility. Again, I am a buyer of BlackRock for the long term.

data.title: Kuehne + Nagel International: Tariff Uncertainty Spells Pain Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Kuehne + Nagel International AG (OTCPK:KHNGF) recently reported its full-year 2024 results, so I wanted to have a look at how it performed throughout the year and give some comments on its outlook and the biggest worry that may impact it in the short run.

The last time I covered the company, I gave it a hold rating and said that it may underperform going forward. Since then, the company’s share price tanked by over 25%. I don’t think this is the bottom yet, and I expect further pain ahead. Hold rating maintained.

Briefly on Performance

Looking at the company’s top line, we can see it had a strong beginning to the year, which started to weaken by the end of the year due to overall weaker demand in the logistics sector. Average yields for the year were down 10%, which contributed to the company’s lackluster top line performance.

Now, moving on to efficiency and profitability, we can see due to lower yields, the company’s margins across the board took a slight hit also. There was some recovery in EBIT and EBITDA margins at the end of the year due to Sea Logistics' underlying improvement of 23% as well as Air Logistics, and Contract Logistics, which saw better yields and better volumes in Q4. The drag on operations was Road Logistics, which experienced persistent headwinds throughout the year caused by volume decline, organic decline in gross profits of 5%, and capacity cost pressures.

Continuing on with other efficiency and profitability metrics, we can see that since there was a slight decline in the overall bottom line, it is not surprising to see a downtrend in the company’s ROA and ROE. Nevertheless, the company still boasts some impressive returns on assets and shareholder equity, even if they have come down slightly. The management is doing a great job at utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital.

Additionally, it seems that the company maintained its competitive edge throughout the year with an impressive return on total capital of around 19.5%, which is head and shoulders above the competition, with ZIM Integrated Shipping Services (ZIM) coming in closer than the rest of the peers. I look for at least 10% in ROTC, which tells me a company has a competitive advantage as well as an economic moat, and KHNGF’s 19.5% ROTC is outstanding.

Moving on to the company’s financial position, the company finished the year with around $600m in cash and equivalents, against zero debt. What else is there to be said about its position if not it’s great! No solvency or liquidity risk here. The company will do fine in any sort of downturn in global economies.

Overall, the company had a decent year in 2024. We can see some mixed results here and there, which didn’t help its top line performance in the end but most of the company’s business segments started to see a recovery in efficiency and profitability by the end of the year, which may signal a turnaround is not too far off, however, all of that will depend on the economic conditions in 2025.

Comments on the Outlook

We can see from the results that the net turnover and EBIT figures show a normalization from the pandemic-driven highs. We can finally put behind that outlier of many businesses, as most of them have passed those numbers already.

Regarding Sea Logistics, the company is going to continue its focus on optimizing operations through the eTouch Program, which is meant to streamline the company’s manual processes and outright reduce them via automation, as well enhancing customer experience through the whole journey, across all touchpoints of the supply chain. Digitization remains one of the key drivers of efficiency for many companies, especially companies like KHNGF. eTouch automates high-volume, commodity freight transactions like booking, tracking, and quoting of shipments, which is going to reduce, and already has reduced operational costs significantly over time. This platform also has enhanced collaboration, for example, the company partners with Air France-KLM Martinair Cargo, which allows it to link directly with individual systems of each business seamlessly. This way the customers benefit from a faster and more accurate booking process, and they also get a lot more visibility throughout the supply chain, which saves a lot of time.

Regarding Air Logistics, the company benefited from strong performance in pharma/healthcare, and perishable sectors, which improved yields and volumes in the latest quarter. These tailwinds are looking to continue into 2025 if nothing changes regarding the economies.

Speaking of the economic outlook, it seems that the shipment sector will be facing overcapacity problems going forward. Many shipment companies built a lot of ships due to the surge in demand for goods during the pandemic, which we can see didn’t last long at all. The next big obstacle for these companies is what to do with all of these ships going forward. Are we going to see growth in demand returning in 2025?

There is a lot of uncertainty regarding the tariffs implemented by the U.S. on the world, with a massive 145% tariff on Chinese goods. China already retaliated and will continue to do so if Trump ups the tariff even more. During his first term, there were some tariffs implemented too, but that didn’t turn out to be as bad as expected since volumes shifted toward other Asian countries as well as the gap left by the U.S. was filled by the European countries.

I don’t think this is going to turn out the same as last time, since the tariffs are much higher now than they were then, which prompts many analysts and economists to predict the U.S. will enter into a recession soon. This means we may see a further decrease in volumes, while overcapacity continues to be a huge factor and will weigh on the company’s profitability. So, being a freight forwarder, overcapacity is not an issue for the company, however, overcapacity in the broader sense will have an indirect negative impact on the company’s operations such as lower freight rates, leading to lower average yield because of the weaker pricing power in a market that is experiencing overcapacity.

Closing Comments

The company is due to report its next results at the end of the month, with revenue estimates to be down 8% sequentially to $6.99B, so it will be interesting to hear what the management has to say about the company’s outlook for 2025 and what they feel is going to happen this year regarding the unprecedented tariff situation. Therefore, I am going to stay on the sidelines here and await more information regarding the uncertainty and how these companies are going to adapt to a new environment going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GE Aerospace: Long-Term Growth, Short-Term Risk
data.description:
GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) is one of the aerospace and defense companies that I have been bullish on. In January, I attached a $239 price target to the name. The stock price, however, has declined 7.8% since then. In comparison, the S&P 500 (SP500) declined 13.7% since then. It is not the kind of performance we are looking for in a name with a buy rating. However, perhaps the lower decline that GE Aerospace showed somewhat reflects its resilience.

In this report, I will discuss the risks that the company faces in the current trade environment. Additionally, I will discuss how the earnings and free cash flow estimates have developed and update my price target for GE.

GE Aerospace Business Leans On Long-Term Trends

GE Aerospace leans on long-term demand for airplanes and thus airplane engines and associated services. Typically, the airplane engines do not come with high margins. However, the services have a very attractive margin over the longer term, as prices for life-limited parts or LLPs typically are escalated at a rate higher than inflation. Through this escalation cadence, GE Aerospace is shielded against inflation, labor cost increases and raw material costs. Aero engine prices are typically also escalated similarly.

While the business is shielded through its escalation principle, tariffs can still increase the cost of goods and a bigger risk is that it can disrupt the aerospace supply chain, which has been fragile for years. Additionally, in the case GDP growth slows down, there might be lower demand for new airplanes. Typically, demand for airplanes is supported by 2-3 percent in GDP growth. GDP growth is expected to be 2.7% in 2025-2026 and that is below the long-term average of 3.1% and the current trade turmoil may adversely impact GDP growth. The European Central Bank already revised its GDP outlook down by 20 basis points in March. Vanguard has lowered the economic growth outlook for the US to below 1% and growth outlooks for China have also been trimmed. So, there is substantial pressure on the GDP outlook that could subsequently result in lower capacity growth for air travel and affect demand for new aero engines and services.

Marginally Lower Estimates For GE Aerospace

For GE Aerospace, we see that EBITDA estimates for 2025 have been lower by 0.6% and 0.8% in the year after. This results in a total downward revision of 0.4% between FY 2025 and FY 2027. Free cash flow is expected to be 1.4% than previously estimated, but 3.7% lower in FY 2026, for a total reduction in FCF estimates of 0.9%. Those are estimate changes that don’t really move the needle in my opinion.

GE Aerospace Has Upside Despite Lower Peer Group Valuation Multiple

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While EBITDA and free cash flow estimates did not come down substantially, I am adjusting my price target from $239 to $206.25. This is not driven by the earnings growth, but by the lower trading multiple for the aerospace and defense industry.

I do believe in the prospects of GE Aerospace, but at this point it is fair to account for the lower EV/EBITDA of the peer group and value accordingly. In that scenario, there remains 14% upside and another 13% in the year after. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating for GE Aerospace.

Conclusion: GE Aerospace Remains Attractive

GE Aerospace is not cheap; it is fairly valued with FY 2025 earnings in mind, and that also was the case in my assessment in January. However, I do believe that the lower stock price does offer an entry position to incrementally add on market weakness with the long-term prospects of the company in mind.

As a result, I am reducing my price target in line with a lower peer group valuation but reiterate my buy rating.

data.title: Amazon Turns Challenges Into Opportunities: Why I Am Buying
data.description:
Investing When The Market Drops

In this article, I want to focus on Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and explain why I bought more of it and will continue doing so. Although some may call it a no-brainer or a screaming buy, I think we should avoid similar ways of saying when we buy shares. It may be easier to come to a buy rating in certain circumstances, but we are, in any case, using our rational intelligence, which we should always consider our best asset. So, I think Amazon well deserves some thoughts, which I want to share in this article.

Whipsaw scenarios dominate the financial scene nowadays. We have seen the market trade down and up double digits in just one day. We have rallies that give some relief to battered bulls, and then bears seem to emerge once again. Who knows where the market is going? Nobody, unless the answer is: somewhere. But we know that the likelihood of the market being far higher ten years from now is considerable.

This answer clashes with what investors want: safety and certainty. And yet, we also know that opportunities arise when the clouds loom on the horizon or, in other words, when risk is perceived. There are plenty of papers and interesting pieces of research on panic selling and how it leads investors to mistakes such as selling assets prematurely, ignoring fundamentals, overreacting, herd behavior, and avoiding re-entering the market. But I think many of us have felt fear in the past few days and have at least thought about selling or exiting the market. After all, if we have been invested for a few years, we are probably sitting on quite large gains that we would like to preserve.

This is when having a strategy and sticking to it pays off. Therefore, even though I have no clue whether the market will head north or south this year, I consider what is happening right now as a buying opportunity.

Now, Aswath Damodaran, to this extent, has argued that "the data seems to point more to a markdown in equity values than to panic selling". He showed how investors respond to a crisis by affecting the price they pay for assets, demanding higher risk premiums. He also explains that deeming every market dip as a buying opportunity can be due to intellectual laziness. What I am doing right now is due to the fact that I have a macroeconomic view that thinks this crisis could lead to a stronger Western bloc. Why I think so can lead me astray from the purpose of this article, but I think North America and Europe will eventually have to be strictly bonded to be of the same size as China and India.

In any case, right now, we could profit from the situation by simply buying the market. But I am picking some stocks I consider to be all-weather options that are currently being erroneously discounted. In any case, it is key to know what we are buying.

Think about the example we have heard so many times: if you buy a nice home you feel comfortable living in, will you sell it if the home market tanks? Probably not. Well, I am currently after stocks. I would like to hold on to, just as I want to hold on to my nice home with a sea view, I was able to buy for my family (here is a picture I took a few days ago at sunrise; having very young kids has the "advantage" of early wake-ups).

Now, enough with behavioral finance and personal examples. What am I buying right now? At the time of writing, I am seeing the market plummet by over 5% after one of its best days, where many stocks gained +10%.

Personally, I believe this is just one of those rallies that are typically seen during bear markets. We are reading all over the place how two similar days with double-digit gains happened in October 2008, as the Great Financial Crisis was spreading.

So, yes. I am buying stocks. But to paint the whole picture, I have to give more information. In the past year and a half, I have stacked up some cash as I was finding it more and more difficult to invest in convincing buys. I kept buying stocks, but it was harder and harder to find good deals. So, I took advantage of 5% yielding savings accounts and increased my deposits. Secondly, I am not deploying in the market everything I saved all at once. This is because bear markets can last a year, but sometimes can be longer. Since no one knows when it will end, it is better to step in gradually and keep consistency in dollar-cost-averaging.

To be fully transparent, I am not buying only Amazon, but I have also been actively buying Ferrari (RACE), I have doubled my position in Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMY), and a new entry in my portfolio: United Rentals (URI). I have not sold a single share of any stock.

Why Amazon

My investment in Amazon began when the release of the first season of The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power was about to be released in September 2022. We were seeing Amazon plummet day after day. I started picking it up at around $120 and bought the sell-off until it bottomed in the $80s. If you want to read something to divert your attention from tariffs and the current market bloodshed, you may find my research interesting. I was trying to figure out why Amazon could deploy well over $1B just for this series. What I found out was something extremely valuable about Amazon's economics. In short, I focused on how Amazon wants to attract new members into its Prime ecosystem, and I showed how this membership program was highly influenced by Costco's business model. Back in 2022, we estimated that every member who signs up for Amazon Prime spends its estimated lifetime value for Amazon was over $1,300. This meant that with just 759k new Prime members, Amazon was obtaining in present lifetime value an amount similar to its investment for the first two seasons. Probably, the new lifetime value of a Prime member is now higher, in addition, Amazon has also raised its membership fee. So, the economics of this ecosystem are probably getting better. In short, I believed I understood why Amazon always wins.

But I also started focusing on Amazon's strive to be a company of inventors. In particular, I showed how it is probably the only company able to challenge Nvidia's dominance in AI chips. The company stays true to the old saying that someone's margin is someone else's opportunity.

However, because of its relentless inventive nature, Amazon's fundamentals don't always please investors. Let's look at its earnings and its FCF. Since the pandemic, for example, we have witnessed two massive investment cycles. The first one started in late 2020 and was meant to increase Amazon's fulfillment capacity with new warehouses, given the rapid surge of its online sales. Investors saw Amazon's FCF go well into the negative (up to a $30B cash burn). Its earnings too plummeted. As a result, Amazon's valuation multiples were extremely poor.

But then Amazon switched into profit mode and rapidly proved to the market that it can easily make over $50B in FCF. The chart would have probably kept exploding if AI hadn't come about. But since Amazon rapidly saw the opportunity of this revolution, it is now investing deeply to build AI-supportive infrastructure and technologies that will be embedded in its ecosystem. The partnership with Anthropic is key to this extent.

Last, but not least, Amazon's profitable AWS business is now supported by a rapidly growing segment, which is Amazon Ads. I consider Amazon to be the best digital advertising company because no other company I know can advertise so effectively.

What about China?

Right now, the issue concerning investors with Amazon is its relationship with China. But I think we often assume it is far larger than we expect. In short, it mainly affects the retail business. As we will see, if the retail business takes a hit, Amazon's profit margins could actually move higher.

As Amazon explains in its 2024 Annual Report (bold is mine):

The People's Republic of China (PRC) and India regulate Amazon's and its affiliates' businesses and operations in country through regulations and license requirements that may restrict (i) foreign investment in and operation of the internet, IT infrastructure, data centers, retail, delivery, and other sectors, (ii) internet content, and (iii) the sale of media and other products and services. For example, in order to meet local ownership, regulatory licensing, and cybersecurity requirements, we provide certain technology services in China through contractual relationships with third parties that hold PRC licenses to provide services. [...] In addition, because China-based sellers account for significant portions of our third-party seller services and advertising revenues, and China-based suppliers provide significant portions of our components and finished goods, regulatory and trade restrictions, data protection and cybersecurity laws, economic factors, geopolitical events, security issues, or other factors negatively impacting China-based sellers and suppliers could adversely affect our operating results.

Amazon doesn't disclose what percentage of its third-party sellers are from China or Hong Kong. I have read several estimates, and they range between 60% to 70%. This is a sensible amount. As we can see, China is more critical to Amazon's supply chain than to its sales. In fact, net sales attributable to countries that represent a significant portion of Amazon's sales don't include China.

Amazon does over two-thirds of its sales in the U.S., which is over 10x the size of the second-largest market, which is Germany. After all, we know that the Chinese market is dominated by Alibaba (BABA).

But let's now understand where this piece of information finds its context.

In 2024, Amazon grew its total revenue by 11% to $638B. It has three segments: North America, International, and AWS. Respectively, they were up 10% to $387B, 9% to $143B, 19% to $108B. Just ten years ago, AWS did only $4.8B, and Amazon's total revenue was below $90B. Adj. Free Cash Flow reached $36.2B.

Now, let's look at the company's net sales by category. Online stores represent $247B. Third-party seller services are the second-largest category, with a revenue of $156B.

Now, Amazon doesn't disclose its margins by category. We know that AWS's margins often exceed 30%. For the retail business, the number we usually consider correct is 6-7%. The ads business can be compared to Google's, which reports an operating margin close to 40%.

Therefore, Amazon is exposed to the current tariff war for two reasons: difficulties in replacing its Chinese suppliers, and lower ad revenue.

However, I think we can also consider that some trends could be triggered to offset part of the impact. In particular, the advertising business could see some growth from new third-party sellers or higher-priced goods sellers that want to reposition themselves and take market share from Chinese suppliers that are pulling back.

There could, however, be the overall impact of a recession. Not even Amazon is immune to this.

Now, what I would like to show is that if third-party sellers diminish, Amazon could take a hit in online sales, but this would make AWS weigh more, making the company more profitable. Moreover, Chinese suppliers could be replaced by other higher-tier suppliers that are affected by lower tariffs. Think about Turkey, for example, or India. There could also be an influx of new suppliers, as demand for many goods manufactured in China is expected to remain. These new suppliers would need to make themselves known on Amazon and could spend on advertising, offsetting the negative impact of fewer Chinese suppliers.

In other words, I think Amazon's ecosystem is so big that it will eventually rebalance its operations.

Valuation

As I said, Amazon's valuation can fluctuate quite a bit due to how Amazon prefers to invest big when it finds an opportunity, rather than outlining a smooth upward trajectory of its earnings. However, right now, we are seeing Amazon invest convincingly while also proving that it is a cash flow-generating machine. As a result, we have some multiples that reflect Amazon's valuation while the company is doing a bit of everything, so to speak.

We have a fwd PE below 30, 28.6 to be precise, which is almost unprecedented. Its fwd EV/EBITDA is around 12, which seems cheap by Amazon's standards. Even its P/FCF is interesting, with a 12.5 multiple. Considering Amazon could well do $70B in FCF if it paused its AI-related capex, we could have this multiple cut in half.

Could Amazon trade down? Yes, but the valuation would only become more and more attractive for a company that is deeply entrenched in the U.S. and the Western world. But, given the structure of its operations, I consider it one of the best safe havens in the market. Although it may be more volatile than defensive stocks, what is truly defensive of Amazon is its ecosystem and the structure of its business, which will help it sail through this crisis as it has with the others, it faced since its founding.

data.title: AMD: Look Beyond Tariffs And Focus On The Big Picture
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) has seen great volatility, but I am of the view that a lot of the AI hype has left the balloon and investors can focus solely on the fundamentals. Wall Street appears to be punishing the stock due to the competitive gap versus Nvidia (NVDA), which appears to only be getting wider and wider. I have no arguments against that narrative, as the numbers do appear to imply that NVDA continues to assert dominance over the GPU market. However, following what I view to be an excessive selloff due to tariffs, at current valuations I’d argue that AMD can remain a distant second and still present a compelling investment. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

AMD Stock Price

I last covered AMD in January, when I named it a top pick even in spite of being “second fiddle.” The stock has retreated nearly 20% since then.

Investors may need to be patient with this one due to the thesis hinging on a shift in sentiment, but I see the numbers eventually validating this story.

AMD Stock Key Metrics

AMD has fallen behind in the generative AI race. This company was once regarded as closely following NVDA, but the gap has now widened beyond repair.

But I’d argue that, given the solid numbers AMD is posting, it is not necessary nor justified to continue the comparisons between the two firms.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 24% YoY revenue growth to $7.7 billion, coming in at the high end of guidance of $7.5 billion (plus or minus $300 million). The company was profitable on a GAAP basis and saw non-GAAP EPS jump 42% YoY due to operating leverage.

The company drove growth primarily from the data center segment, which makes up more than half of overall revenues. Data center revenues grew 69% YoY and 9% sequentially. These are great numbers, but greatly lag the 93% YoY and 15% QoQ growth seen at NVDA. The company continued to see weakness in the gaming and embedded segments, but as management signaled in the third quarter, I expect these to see a pronounced cyclical recovery in addition to lapping easier comparables.

AMD ended the quarter with $5.1 billion of cash versus $1.7 billion of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has guided for roughly $7.1 billion in revenue in the first quarter, representing 30% YoY growth and a 7% sequential decline. I suspect that some investors may have been disappointed by the latter point. While AMD has historically seen seasonal declines between these quarters, a true “Nvidia moment” would have presumably negated any seasonality.

On the conference call, management noted that:

IBM, Digital Ocean, Vultr and several other AI-focused CSPs have begun deploying AMD Instinct accelerators for new instances.

I think the fact that management is highlighting (in my opinion) tier 2 cloud operator customers sums up the competitive landscape quite well. Management appeared to give soft guidance of expanding their AI data center revenues from $5 billion in 2024 to “tens of billions of dollars of annual revenue over the coming years.” They repeated that target several times on the call, including as a response to an analyst question with regard to whether they can grow these revenues by 60% in 2025 as well. My guess is that management is (rightfully so) trying to manage analyst expectations for their AI business, which is unlikely to follow the same trajectory as that of NVDA.

Is AMD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, AMD traded hands at just over 20x this year’s earnings.

That looks like a cheap multiple given consensus estimates calling for solid double-digit growth over the coming years.

I'd note that estimates might prove optimistic in the near term due to blowback from tariffs, even if AMD might not be so directly affected given the exclusion of semiconductor chips from tariffs. I would not be surprised to see IT spending pullback in the near term due to heightened uncertainty.

On the other hand, I view the growth of AI, data, and all things data center to be unstoppable over the long term. Analyst expectations call for net margins to hit around 31% by 2029, which looks quite conservative in my view. With the stock trading at a price to earnings growth ratio ("PEG ratio") under 1x, it seems to me that Wall Street is pricing in the possibility that the growth story gets disrupted. I view this as unlikely as I can always see a market for lower-cost GPUs. The presence of such a formidable competitor might put a ceiling on valuation multiples, but I see a case for this stock to re-rate to around a 1.2x to 1.5x PEG ratio, equating to around 24x to 30x earnings or 7.2x to 9x sales (based on a 30% long-term net margin assumption).

AMD Stock Risks

If the AI story on the whole falls apart, then AMD can see material financial impact. While it is unclear if AMD will see more or less impact than NVDA in such a scenario, I think it will very likely see considerable impact regardless. It is possible that AMD really is being disrupted by NVDA and that growth will be far lower than analysts expect. In this case, I would expect the stock to re-rate downward due to it earning a more cyclical versus secular multiple. I expect AMD stock to face considerable volatility in a recession, but the solid GAAP profitability may give it the financial firepower to repurchase stock at lower levels. I am of the view that a lot of the pessimism has already been priced into the stock, as the recent volatility might not imply the same moving forward.

AMD Stock Conclusion

In comparison with NVDA, AMD is clearly struggling to benefit from anything close to the same growth seen in AI. Even so, the company is still posting respectable growth rates alongside GAAP profitability and a net cash balance sheet. I argue that the low valuation means that the company can underperform NVDA and still allow for strong upside ahead. AMD's strong top-line growth rates, net cash balance sheet, and undervaluation explain why it is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Roche: Forget Tariffs And Focus On The Positives -- There Are Many
data.description:
Investment Overview

The Trump administration's tariffs came, created mayhem, and then took 90 days off — at least the more severe levies, and excepting China. It has been one of the more bizarre economic policies, implemented on "Liberation Day", and swiftly withdrew as stock markets globally began to crash.

The pharmaceuticals industry has been protected from tariffs for over 30 years thanks to an agreement orchestrated by the World Trade Organisation ("WTO") but the President insisted a couple of days ago their exemption was about to come to an end in the US.

President Trump has been quoted as saying they will arrive "very soon", and allegedly told reporters they would be "at a level that you haven't really seen before", and may have suggested they will be >25%. The rationale for tariffs on Pharma? According to President Trump:

We’re going to put tariffs on our pharmaceuticals and once we do that, they’re going to come rushing back into our country because we’re the big market.

The advantage we have over everybody is that we’re the big market. So we’re going to be announcing very shortly a major tariff on pharmaceuticals.

Unsurprisingly, Pharma stocks have been tanking as a result of the tariff threat - my research suggests -8% on a one-week basis, and -13% on a one-month basis across the fifteen or so largest.

Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY) shares are down nearly 20% on a one-month basis, and the company's market cap has dropped <$200bn as a result, which is somewhat low for a company that generated $68.7bn of revenues last year, operating income of $21.6bn, and earnings per share of $11.45 on a GAAP basis, and $20.3 on an adjusted basis, which translates to a price to earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~12x.

Given the ephemeral nature of the tariffs imposed on countries, however, should the Pharma industry be genuinely fearful of a >25% tariff on exports to the US, or should we instead think about how cheap European Pharma stocks are today and consider this a good time to buy? In this post, I'll tease out some ways to think about the opportunity — or lack of it.

Is A Tariff On Pharma Exports To The US Genuinely Realistic?

There's no question that European Pharmas are concerned about the imposition and impact of tariffs on exports to the US. Almost as soon as the news broke on Tuesday evening that Pharmas may be next in line for a levy, The European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations (EFPIA), of which Roche is a member, alongside other Euro Pharma giants such as Novartis AG (NVS), Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), GSK plc (GSK), and US Pharmas including e.g., Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), Pfizer Inc. (PFE), and Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) met with the European Commission president, Ursula von der Leyen, and issued a press release stating that:

unless Europe delivers rapid, radical policy change then pharmaceutical research, development, and manufacturing are increasingly likely to be directed towards the US

The press release added:

The US now leads Europe on every investor metric from availability of capital, intellectual property, and speed of approval to rewards for innovation. In addition to the uncertainty created by the threat of tariffs, there is little incentive to invest in the EU and significant drivers to relocate to the US.
